reasoning: Valaki (someone) is subject; valakit (-t accusative) is object; megvert = beat. So “Someone beat someone.”
Kit = whom (object question); valaki = someone (subject). “Whom did someone beat?” → “Who got beaten by someone?”
Senki nem = no one didn’t → no one did; a Petyát has -t accusative = Peter (object). “No one beat Peter.”
Valakit (object) is fronted; senki nem vert meg = no one beat. ⇒ “There is someone who didn’t get beaten.”
Senki nem = no one; mindenkit (everyone-ACC). ⇒ “No one beat everyone.”
Senkit (no one-ACC) fronted object; a Petya = Peter (subject). ⇒ “Peter beat no one.”
Ki = who (subject); nem … senkit = didn’t … anyone. ⇒ “Who didn’t beat anyone?”
Valaki = someone (subject); senkit nem = didn’t … anyone. ⇒ “There’s someone who didn’t beat anyone.”
Mindenki = everyone (subject); valakit (someone-ACC) as object. ⇒ “Everyone beat someone.”
Valaki = someone; a Marcit (Marci-ACC) object; megverte = beat. ⇒ “Someone beat Martin.”
Senkit (anyone-ACC) … senki (no one) with nem → “No one beat anyone,” equivalent to “No one got beaten.”
Kit = whom (object question); … senki = no one (subject). ⇒ “Whom did no one beat?” = “Who wasn’t beaten by anyone?”
First-person verb vertem; nem … senkit = didn’t … anyone. ⇒ “I didn’t beat anyone.”
